coredata.dc:title: Tuning composite nanofiltration membranes with γ-cyclodextrin for improved Mg2+/Li+ selectivity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Nan
Zhang, Tiancan
Xue, Weihao
Zhao, Ying
Zhu, Bo
Pei, Xiaoyuan
Xu, Zhiwei
coredata.dc:description: As a new frontier method, nanofiltration (NF) membranes still face challenges in achieving a balance between selectivity and permeability, especially in the field of extracting lithium from brines. In the study, we addressed this issue by incorporating γ-cyclodextrins (CDs) with amphipathic cavities onto the polyamide chains using interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, employing polyethyleneimine (PEI) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). The addition of γ-cyclodextrin (γ-CD) enhances the hydrophilicity and water flux of NF membrane on account of its remarkable structural properties. The outer surface of γ-CD exhibits hydrophilic characteristics, while its inner cavity possesses hydrophobic properties. Benefiting from its water-attracting surface and water-repellent cavity, γ-CD could enhance the overall water affinity and water permeability of the NF membrane. Furthermore, the dimensions of the γ-CD cavity allowed for effective screening Mg2+ / Li+ to a certain extent. Notably, γ-CD exhibited a superior capability to accommodate and encapsulate Mg2+ ions, indicating a higher binding affinity compared to Li+. Thus, Li+ was easier to pass through the NF membrane grafted by γ-CDs than Mg2+. The optimized NF membrane demonstrated an enhanced magnesium-lithium separation coefficient of 23.61, 237 % better than that of the TFC-control membrane. This work introduces a novel approach for designing and constructing of high-permselectivity NF membranes for Mg2+ / Li+ separation utilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: γ-cyclodextrin
Mg2+/Li+ Separation
Nanofiltration membrane
Separation mechanism
Host–guest interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Binding affinity of γ-CD for Mg2+ and Li+ ions
Analysis of the morphology of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane surface
Investigation of element composition and functional group distribution on the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane
Evaluation of hydrophilic properties and surface charge appraisal of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane
Evaluating the separation efficiency of γ-CD-PEI-TMC NF membrane
Pore size distribution of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane
Mechanisms of separation in the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We express our profound gratitude to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22108203) for their significant financial support, which immensely contributed to the successful completion of this research project. Furthermore, we express our gratitude to the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for providing access to their cutting-edge facilities, including Scanning Electron Microscope, and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy, which significantly facilitated our experimental analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram illustrating the preparation process of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC NF membrane.
SEM depictions of varying γ-CDs concentrations on themembrane surface: (a) TFC, (b) TFC-1, (c) TFC-2, (d) TFC-3, (e) TFC-4, (f) TFC-5.
Cross-sectional SEM visuals of membranes with different concentrations of γ-CDs: (a) TFC, (b) TFC-1, (c) TFC-2, (d) TFC-3, (e) TFC-4, (f) TFC-5.
Three-dimensional AFM height topographical illustrations of the membrane surface subjected to various γ-CDS concentrations: (a) TFC-1, (b) TFC-2, (c) TFC-3, (d) TFC-4, (e) TFC-5.
FTIR spectra of the base material PES, along with the membranes TFC, TFC-1, TFC-2, TFC-3, TFC-4 and TFC-5.
XPS spectra fitting visuals: (a-c) for the TFC membrane, (d-f) for the TFC-2 membrane, with the columns indicating convoluted high-resolution of C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively.
(a) Water contact angles and (b) zeta potential of surface for each fabricated membrane.
Separation efficacy of membranes prepared with varied γ-CD concentrations.
Long term stability of TFC-2 membrane.
(a) MWCO of two membranes and (b) pore size distribution of two membranes.
Diagram illustrating the separation mechanism of Mg2+ / Li+.
Membranes and their respective γ-CDs concentrations.
Examination of surface roughness analysis of TFC and TFC1-5 membranes using average roughness (Ra) and root-mean-squared roughness (Rq).
Comparative evaluation of the performance evaluation of various NF membranes reported in previous studies for Li+ and Mg2+ ion separation.

head.ce:title: Tuning composite nanofiltration membranes with γ-cyclodextrin for improved Mg2+/Li+ selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textiles Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Textiles Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textiles Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Chemistry, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Chemistry, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new frontier method, nanofiltration (NF) membranes still face challenges in achieving a balance between selectivity and permeability, especially in the field of extracting lithium from brines. In the study, we addressed this issue by incorporating γ-cyclodextrins (CDs) with amphipathic cavities onto the polyamide chains using interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, employing polyethyleneimine (PEI) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). The addition of γ-cyclodextrin (γ-CD) enhances the hydrophilicity and water flux of NF membrane on account of its remarkable structural properties. The outer surface of γ-CD exhibits hydrophilic characteristics, while its inner cavity possesses hydrophobic properties. Benefiting from its water-attracting surface and water-repellent cavity, γ-CD could enhance the overall water affinity and water permeability of the NF membrane. Furthermore, the dimensions of the γ-CD cavity allowed for effective screening Mg2+ / Li+ to a certain extent. Notably, γ-CD exhibited a superior capability to accommodate and encapsulate Mg2+ ions, indicating a higher binding affinity compared to Li+. Thus, Li+ was easier to pass through the NF membrane grafted by γ-CDs than Mg2+. The optimized NF membrane demonstrated an enhanced magnesium-lithium separation coefficient of 23.61, 237 % better than that of the TFC-control membrane. This work introduces a novel approach for designing and constructing of high-permselectivity NF membranes for Mg2+ / Li+ separation utilization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium, as a kind of the rare element, is widely regarded as “energy-critical element” for batteries, nuclear energy, aerospace and other fields on account of its exceptional physical and chemical properties [1–3]. In order to alleviate the shortage of lithium resources, extensive researchers have been devoted to endeavors focused on lithium extraction from brine [4,5]. However, this recovery of lithium method suffered from low separation efficiency induced by the close hydration diameters of Mg2+ (0.86 nm) and Li+ (0.76 nm), as well as the high proportion of Mg2+/Li+ (40–1837) [6,7]. To date, the conventional technologies for lithium extraction from salt lakes such as adsorption [8], solvent, precipitation [9], extraction [10], and ion exchange [11] were considered environmentally unfriendly, energy-intensive, relatively costly, and preparation-difficult [12–14]. Therefore, appropriate methods for magnesium-lithium separation in salt lakes are urgently required.

Compared to the traditional technologies, nanofiltration (NF) membrane have emerged as a promising approach for magnesium-lithium separation, offering several advantages such as environmental friendliness, cost-effectiveness, energy efficiency, simplicity, and excellent ion separation capabilities [15]. Generally, NF membranes typically comprise an active layer and its support structure, with the selective layer being crucial for achieving desirable separation performance. In particular, the creation of thin-membrane composite (TFC) polyamide membranes using interfacial polymerization (IP) on porous support ultrafiltration membranes has gained considerable interest and has been extensively employed in various industrial applications [16,17]. Nevertheless, the trade-off effect between selectivity and permeability remains a limiting factor in NF processes [18,19]. To address this challenge, various advanced membrane approaches have emerged, aiming to control the pore structure of membranes and manipulate the surface charge properties. These approaches are based on the fundamental separation mechanisms of nanofiltration (NF) membranes, including the steric hindrance effect and Donnan exclusion theory [20,21]. By regulating these factors, it is possible to strengthen the efficiency of NF membranes in separation for the recovery of lithium. In recent years, the optimization of pore size and structure of the active layer had been achieved through the incorporation of novel porous nanomaterials, containing metal organic framework (MOF) materials [22], covalent framework (COF) materials [23], and crown ether [24], which offer uniform and fixed channel sizes. Despite the outstanding enhancement in permeability and selectivity abilities of TFC NF membranes achieved by involving these porous nanomaterials, challenges remain in terms of their structural stability and the tendency of nanomaterials to agglomerate at high loading levels. Therefore, it is crucial to identify a suitable hydrophilic nanomaterial with intrinsic pores that can effectively control the pore environment while maintaining good dispersion characteristics.

Cyclodextrins (CDs) are macrocyclic oligosaccharides connected by α-1, 4-glycosidic bonds and are widely recognized as suitable materials for NF membrane preparation due to their appropriate cavity sizes, amphipathic cavity properties, host–guest recognition abilities, and low cost effectiveness. Among the various types of CDs, α-, β-, and γ-CDs are widely employed. These CDs are composed of D-glucose units, forming macrocyclic rings with fixed angstrom-sized inner cavities. The unique three-dimensional toroidal shape of CDs enables them to possess a hydrophilic outer wall and a hydrophobic cavity enriched with hydroxyl groups [5,25]. Therefore, CDs are well dispersed in aqueous solution even under high concentration to prevent agglomeration in NF membranes for improvement of membrane performance. Additionally, the hydrophilic exterior of CDs facilitates efficient interactions between hydroxyl groups and water molecules, while the hydrophobic interior of CD’s cavity serves as a fast transmission channel for water molecules [26,27]. Thus, these properties jointly indicate that CDs bearing amphipathic characteristics can efficiently enhance water flux of membranes. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the fixed-size cavity of CDs is applied to sieve molecules or ions based on regioselective effect. In brief, the intrinsic cavity of cyclodextrins (CDs) acts as a host for forming complexes with a guest of appropriate dimensions through a range of intermolecular weak interactions, containing hydrophobic forces, electrostatic interactions, van der Waals interactions, and hydrogen bonding [28–30]. For instance, previous studies have demonstrated the successful utilization of TMC and β-CDs in an IP reaction on a vermiculite lamellar layer to fabricate a hydrophilic TFC nanofiltration membrane. This membrane demonstrated exceptional separation performance for xylene isomers, showcasing its efficient separation performance [29]. Among commonly used CDs, the cavity diameter of γ-CDs (0.76–0.83 nm) falls within the size range of Mg2+ (0.86 nm) and Li+ (0.76 nm), making it particularly suitable for applications in the field of separating magnesium and lithium. Furthermore, due to its adaptable and flexible cavity, γ-CDs offer the added advantage of effectively binding metal cations through non-covalent interactions. Moreover, due to its adaptable and flexible cavity, γ-CDs offer the advantages of binding metal cations through non-covalent interactions. The binding modes and behaviors of γ-CD-cation complexes can be evaluated through using a combination of computational techniques and experimental methods. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations offer significant findings into the molecular interactions within the complexes, while the polarizable continuum model (PCM) computations offer a reliable framework to understand the solvation effects. Additionally, electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) enables identification and analysis of the γ-CD-cation complexes, providing experimental evidence to support the computational findings. The binding strength of γ-CD-cation complexes depends on the charge density and electronic configuration of metal cation, as well as the coordination number sites [30–32]. Therefore, the key determinant of γ-CD-cation interaction is the properties of metal cation itself, which possess higher charged density towards more intensive affinity with γ-CD [30]. As a result, despite the use of γ-CDs in magnesium-lithium separation was only reported in our previous research [33], γ-CDs can have a pivotal impact on enhancing the selectivity of Mg2+ ions and Li+, thereby enhancing the overall separation effectiveness of NF membranes.

In the present study, we employed the IP reaction using PEI and TMC as the starting materials. Subsequently, γ-CDs interacted with the remaining carboxyl groups and acyl chloride groups in the polyamide chain, facilitating the fabrication of the composite membrane. The application of this novel approach led to the creation of a compact and stable active layer within the composite NF membrane. Unlike our previous work [33], the composite NF membrane in this study featured an abundance of γ-CDs on its surface, enabling precise and effective screening of Mg2+ and Li+ ions. Furthermore, a comprehensive investigation was conducted to examine the influence of γ-CDs on various aspects including morphology, chemical structure, hydrophilicity, stability, as well as the composite NF membranes permeation and separation efficiency of ions of Mg2+ and Li+. Moreover, we conducted an extensive analysis using various analytical techniques to investigate both the formation mechanism and permselectivity mechanism of the composite NF membrane. The work aims to offer theoretical basis and technical support for Mg2+ and Li+ separation membrane.

N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Polyethersulfone (PES) with a molecular weight of 62,000 g/mol, and polyethylene glycol (Mw = 10,000 g/mol) were provided by Tianjin Tongsheng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Wuxi, China). PEI (Mw = 70,000 g/mol, 50 wt%) was acquired by Jinhuitaiya Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The γ-CDs used in this study (98 % purity) were provided from Boer Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Prior to use, the γ-CDs underwent a recrystallization process using water and were subjected to vacuum drying at 90 °C for 24 h to ensure their purity. For the fabrication of the functional layer using IP reaction, n-hexane and TMC were provided from Runyou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). MgCl2, LiCl, and NaCl, provided from Runyou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China), were employed to make diverse feed solutions. The experimental feed solution consisted of MgCl2 and LiCl, maintaining an approximate Mg2+ to Li+ mass ratio of 30 and a concentration of 2000 ppm. Deuterium oxide, supplied by Jinan Mina Technology Co., Ltd., was employed in the experimental setup.

The porous membranes made of PES used in this study were prepared as a support layer using non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS), as reported in prior literature [34–36]. PEG (10 wt%) and PES polymer (17 wt%) of porogenic agent were dissolved in DMF (73 wt%). Subsequently, the glass plates coated with the polymer solution were promptly submerged in a coagulation bath (purified water) for the process of phase separation at a temperature of 25 °C to initiate phase separation. Following the detachment of the membranes from the glass plates, they were immersed in purified water for an entire day (24 h) to eliminate any residual solvent prior to proceeding to the subsequent stages.

The PA layer was formed using a conventional IP reaction. To begin with, the soaked PES-based membrane was placed within polytetrafluoroethylene frames, ensuring the front side up, and the surface water was gently wiped away using absorbent paper. The aqueous solution of PEI (0.5 wt%) was subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min. After sonication, the solution was evenly spread on the membrane surface and allowed to penetrate for approximately 5 min for complete coverage. Following that, any excess solution was delicately removed with a rubber roller, followed by applying a hexane-based organic solution containing 0.1 wt% TMC to initiate the IP reaction. After 3 min of contact, the excess solution was drained. Then a specific quantity of aqueous γ-CD solution was applied to the membrane surface and kept for 30 min to ensure sufficient wetting before draining the excess solution. Afterward, the sample was subjected to an air-drying process at 60 °C in an oven for 15 min to evaporate hexane and initiate thermal cross-linking. The dried membrane was stored in distilled water for subsequent utilization.

Fig. 1 depicts a schematic diagram illustrating the process, highlighting the essential stages involved. The membranes obtained from the process were labeled as TFC, TFC-1, TFC-2, TFC-3, TFC-4, and TFC-5, with their corresponding γ-CD concentrations documented in Table 1 .

The microscopic structure (surface and cross-section) of the membrane was gleaned via scanning electron microscope (SEM, Geminutesi SEM500, Germany) to observe its microscopic morphology. To assess the surface roughness, an atomic force microscope (AFM, Icon, USA) was employed. An X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was utilized to explore the chemical bonds and elemental composition present on the membrane surface via the D8ADV ANCE instrument (Germany). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, USA) was applied to elucidate the specific chemical groups present on the surface of membrane. The measurement of the membrane surface's zeta potential was accomplished through the use of an electrokinetic analyzer (SurPASSTM3 Anton Paar). A contact angle goniometer (JC2000D1, China) was deployed to assess the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface. The binding constant between γ-CD and Mg2+ and Li+ was determined using 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (AVANCE NEO 600 M, Antompa, Austria).

The nanofiltration membrane's pure water flux was assessed under room temperature conditions using flat-sheet cross-flow tests. The assessment of the composite membrane's separation efficiency and performance in selectively rejecting mixed salt solutions was conducted through the utilization of inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP-OES, PQ9000, Germany). To ensure consistent experimental conditions, all permeability and rejection tests were conducted using standardized parameters, including an operating pressure of 4 bar, a feed flow rate of 115 L⋅h−1, and a set temperature at 25℃. Before the commencement of the tests, the membranes underwent a pre-conditioning process with deionized water at a pressure of 4.5 bar for 30 min to establish their stability. To enhance accuracy, each test was conducted thrice to ensure reliability, and the mean values were calculated to reduce potential errors. The water flux of the membranes (F, L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) was estimated using the subsequent formula:
(1)
F =
V
A × t × Δ p

The volume of water (V, in liters) permeating through the membrane within the sample collection time (t, in hours), the effective membrane filtration area (A, in square meters), and the trans-membrane pressure (Δp, in bar) are used in the calculation of permeate flux. Similarly, the nanofiltration membrane's ability to reject salt was assessed using the same experimental setup. The salt rejection rate (R) was calculated using the subsequent formula:
(2)
R =
1 -
C p
C f
× 100 %

The feed concentration (Cf ) and permeate concentration (Cp ) are utilized in the calculation of the separation factor (SMg,Li ) to assess the NF membrane's selective separation efficacy between Li+ and Mg2+. SMg,Li is determined using the subsequent formula:
(3)
S
Li , M g
=
C
Li , p
/
C
Mg , p
C
Li , f
/
C
Mg , f
Here, the concentrations of Mg2+ and Li+ in the permeate (CMg,p and CLi,p , respectively), and in the feed solution (CMg,p and CLi,p , respectively), are taken into account.
To ensure a comprehensive assessment of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC NF membrane's stability, a long-term filtration test was conducted.

The pore size distribution (PSD) of the TFC membrane was analyzed using its molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) value. To succinctly detail the methodology, an aqueous medium with 200 ppm neutral organic compounds, specifically PEG with a molecular weight ranging from 200 to 1000 g mol−1, was employed to determine the membrane's rejection capabilities for varied molecular weights. These assessments were carried out at ambient temperature, under a 5.0 bar transmembrane pressure, covering an effective membrane expanse of around 7.065 cm2. The effective rejection rate (Rs ) for the solutes was deduced using formula (4). In this equation, Cf represents solute concentration in the feed and Cp in the permeate. A TOC analyzer (Multi N/C 2100, Analytikjena) facilitated the analyses. The MWCO is pinpointed by the molecular weight of PEG at which a 90 % rejection rate is observed.
(4)
R S
=
1 -
C p
C f
× 100 %

The correlation between the Stokes diameter (ds , nm) of neutral solutes and their molecular weight (Mw , g mol−1) is captured by formula (5) [37].
(5)
d s
= 33.46 × 1
0
- 3

M
w
0.557

Subsequently, plotting the rejection (R) against PEG's molecular weight gives a fitted curve. When the rejection rate is 50 %, the membrane pore size (μp , nm) mirrors the diameter pore size (dp , nm). The geometric standard deviation (σp ) derives from the solute diameter ratio at rejection rates of 84.13 % and 50 %. The overall PSD was further assessed using formula (6) [3]:
(6)

To ensure a comprehensive assessment of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC NF membrane's stability, a long-term filtration test was conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding affinity of γ-CD for Mg2+ and Li+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The Ka value (the binding constant) for the inclusion complex can be determined using 1H NMR data. The expression for the Ka value when the host–guest forms a 1:1 inclusion is given by the following equilibrium:
(7)
C + S ⇔ C S

The binding constant (Ka) can be represented as follows:
(8)
Ka =
[ C S ]
[ C ] [ S ]
where [CS] represents the equilibrium concentration of the inclusion complex, [C] and [S] represent the concentrations of free γ-CD and S at equilibrium, respectively. Using C0 and S0 to represent the total concentrations of γ-CD and guest, respectively, we have:
(9)
C 0
= C + C S
(10)
S 0
= S + C S

Substituting Eqs. (6) and (7) into (5) gives:
(11)

represents the observed chemical shifts obtained from the NMR spectra.

The value of the equilibrium constant Ka can be solved from Eq. (16) [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the morphology of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane surface
ce:section.ce:para: An SEM assessment was carried out to scrutinize the cross-sectional and surface morphological architectures of the constructed NF membranes. In Fig. 2 , the typical surface properties of the traditional NF membranes, formulated with PEI as the water-phase monomer, exhibited a disc-like PA layer formation that is congruent with previous literature [33,39–42]. This particular morphology arises due to the migration of PEI from the aqueous medium to the non-aqueous medium throughout the fabrication procedure of the membrane, resulting in the observed structure. Fig. 2 also depicts that the structural characteristics of the membrane's surface containing γ-CD differed from that of the conventional polyamide NF membrane. With the incremental addition of γ-CD, a noticeable decrease in the disc-shaped pattern was detected on the surface of all TFC membranes. This alteration can be ascribed to the presence of γ-CD and the reaction between TMC and γ-CD, leading to the formation of a polyester network. In comparison to the original NF membrane's polyamide structure, the polyester structure demonstrated a more uniform and less rough surface.

An in-depth investigation of the cross-section of the nanofiltration membrane (presented in Fig. 3 ) shed more light on the correlation between the thickness of the functional layer and the incorporation amount of γ-CDs, uncovering pivotal data about their interaction. The incorporation of γ-CDs seemed to contribute to a reduction in the thickness of the functional layer, as observed during this analysis. In the absence of γ-CDs, the active layer thickness was measured at 110.16 nm. With the γ-CD content ranging from 0.1 wt% to 0.7 wt%, the slight reduction in membrane thickness actually remains within measurement tolerances. However, it's pertinent to note that when the γ-CD content increases to 0.9 wt%, the membrane thickness does indeed rise significantly to 87.54 nm. This pronounced increase in thickness can be ascribed to the influence of γ-CD at higher concentrations on the membrane structure. With elevated concentrations of γ-CD, it might induce more cross-linking reactions between the polymers, leading to a denser and more uniform polymer network. This enhanced cross-linking is likely the primary contributor to the increased membrane thickness, as such reactions could make the membrane structure more compact and robust.

The surface roughness of the membranes was evaluated through AFM examination, as depicted in Fig. 4
,
Fig. S1
, and Table 2 . A reduction in surface roughness was observed for TFC-2, TFC-3, and TFC-4 membranes when in comparison to the TFC membrane. Notably, the TFC-4 membrane displayed the lowest roughness with an average roughness (Ra) value of 52.3 nm. The findings on roughness were consistent with the observations from SEM images. The surface morphology and structure can be ascribed to the subsequent factors. During the polymerization reaction, the rapid diffusion and reaction of PEI molecules in conjunction with TMC at the binary phase boundary led to the formation of oligomers [43]. Subsequently, the residual acyl chloride groups then interacted with the hydroxyl groups of γ-CD present in the membrane, leading to the creation of a denser active layer, accounting for its compact configuration. Even with a γ-CD concentration as low as 0.1 wt%, the visibility of the apparent disc structure on the membrane surface be accounted for by the relatively low γ-CD concentration. The compact active layer serves as an effective barrier against solute back-diffusion towards the drawing solution, while the unblemished membrane surface promotes the efficient transport of water and solute molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of element composition and functional group distribution on the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The active groups present on the NF membrane’s exterior were explored using FTIR spectral analysis. Remarkably, when γ-CD was grafted to the membrane surface, a chemical link formed between the γ-CD's functional groups (hydroxyl groups) and the molecules of TMC and polyamide oligomers. This association led to the emergence of new polyester structures during the subsequent IP process. Fig. 5 illustrates the FTIR curves of the NF membranes, which differed from those of PES-based membranes. All the NF membranes displayed a prominent peak (1650 cm−1) in the FTIR spectra, which can be attributed ascribed to the existence of the carbonyl group formed during the IP reaction of TMC and PEI [44]. Additionally, the FTIR spectra revealed a distinct peak (1030 cm−1), suggesting the existence of the ester group OCO. A wide-ranging peak at 3350 cm−1 indicated the overlapping stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups (OH) and amine groups (NH), which can be attributed to the additional γ-CDs. Notably, the intensity of these absorbance peaks exhibited an increase with higher γ-CD density.

The surface of the membrane was analyzed for chemical bonding properties and elemental composition using the XPS technique. The chemical bond content, obtained from high-resolution XPS spectra analysis of the membrane surface, specifically focusing on C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks, was employed to assess the degree of crosslinking [45]. Fig. 6 demonstrates the decomposition of the carbon spectrum of γ-CD-PEI-TMC membranes, revealing distinct components corresponding to different chemical bonds. These include CC, CN, CO, OCO, and NCO, which have respective binding energies of 284.8, 285.7, 286.3, 288.9, and 287.7 eV. The existence of OCO groups on the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane surface indicated that the grafting of γ-CD occurred via IP reaction between γ-CD and TMC. In terms of nitrogen species, three distinct peaks were identified at 399.1, 401.3, and 400.0 eV, representing CNH2, NHn + (n = 2, 3), and N-C = O respectively. Notably, the relative abundance of –NHn + (n = 2, 3) exhibited an increase from 13.84 % in the conventional PEI-TMC NF membrane to 19.45 % in the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane. When γ-CD is introduced into the PEI-TMC membrane, potential hydrogen bonding interactions can occur between the hydroxyl groups of γ-CD and the amine groups of PEI, given the multiple hydroxyl groups in γ-CD and multiple amine groups in PEI. This hydrogen bonding might hinder some of the –NH2 groups from reacting with TMC, as their interaction with γ-CD's hydroxyl groups could restrict their chemical reactivity with TMC. Consequently, upon the addition of γ-CD, more –NH2 or –NH groups are retained in the membrane. The presence of peaks at 401.3 eV for –NHn + (n = 2, 3) groups indicated the presence of –NH3 + or –NH2 + species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of hydrophilic properties and surface charge appraisal of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
The charge characteristics of membranes surface significantly influences absorption and repulsion of charged entities in the solution [46,47]. As a result, the influence of γ-CD on the membrane's surface hydrophilicity was evaluated using a static water contact angle tester. Fig. 7
(a) presents the changes in contact angle on the membrane surface corresponding to varying concentrations of γ-CD. As the γ-CD content increased gradually from 0.1 wt% to 0.9 wt%, the water contact angle on the membrane surface gradually diminished. At a γ-CD concentration of 0.9 wt%, the contact angle dropped to a minimum value of 18.5°. The reason for this phenomenon is the high volume of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups in γ-CD, the water contact angle for nanofiltration membranes treated with γ-CD was significantly smaller than that of TFC membranes. This substantial improvement in hydrophilicity by γ-CD enhances the affinity of the active layer for water molecules, facilitating water transport and reducing surface contamination on the membrane.

Furthermore, the membranes' electrical charge characteristics were determined by measuring the zeta potential spanning a pH spectrum from 3.0 to 10.0, as presented in Fig. 7 (b). As the pH of the solution increased, a consistent trend was observed across all membranes, with their surface potential transitioning from positive to negative. Furthermore, in neutral solutions the membrane surfaces exhibited weak electropositivity. The TFC membrane surface inherently possesses a positive charge. Conversely, γ-CD introduces a negative charge due to the presence of its hydroxyl groups. Thus, when γ-CD is grafted onto the TFC membrane's surface, it imparts its negative charge, leading to a decrease in the membrane's overall positive potential. This finding validates the cross-linking reaction between γ-CD and TMC in the nanofiltration membrane's functional layer creation, indicating their chemical interaction. Earlier studies have demonstrated that membranes with electrically neutral surfaces exhibit improved anti-fouling properties [48,49]. Thus, the incorporation of γ-CD could reinforce the performance of the membrane in various ways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the separation efficiency of γ-CD-PEI-TMC NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The establishment of a functional layer through PEI and γ-CD polymerization with TMC significantly influences the separation efficiency of the NF membrane. Previous studies [33,39] indicates that the best thermal processing condition e for obtaining the desired properties is 60 °C. Hence, a thermal processing temperature of 60 °C was employed in this study with the aim to optimize the NF membrane's performance further. The NF membrane without γ-CD exhibited a permeation flux of 3.15 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1 and a SLi, Mg of 7.15 as depicted in Fig. 8 . In general, the water permeation flux of the γ-CD-modified membranes exhibited a slight decrease in comparison to the TFC membrane. It was credited to the formation of a polyester structure through the reaction between γ-CD and the unreacted chloride groups of TMC, which somewhat increased the mass transfer distance for water molecules and solutes to some extent. Besides, the credited to the introduction of γ-CD strengthens the cross-linking in the PA layer, leading to a reduction in membrane pore size. As shown in Fig. 10 (b), explicitly depicts this change in pore size. Given the reduction in pore size, even with the augmented hydrophilicity, the permeate flux might experience a slight decline, though the overall change is minimal. Thus, while the grafting of γ-CD amplifies hydrophilicity, facilitating water molecule transport, the ensuing changes to the PA layer structure cause a modest decrease in permeate flux.

Nevertheless, one must emphasize that the incorporation of γ-CD onto the surface of the active layer in the NF membrane effectively served as a selective barrier for magnesium and lithium ions due to the specific cavity size of γ-CD, functioning as a molecular sieve. As a result, the nanofiltration membrane grafted with γ-CD exhibited a considerably improved separation performance for magnesium and lithium ions compared to the TFC membrane. Notably, at a γ-CD content of 0.3 wt%, the SLi, Mg value peaked at 23.61, demonstrating the effective sieving of Li+ and Mg2+ by the size of γ-CD cavity [11,50].

Membrane durability is a crucial factor in determining their overall performance. However, prolonged use of the TFC NF membrane can result in alterations to the pore morphology due to the swelling of the PA layer, leading to a reduction in selectivity and permeability. Additionally, the accumulation of high-valent ions within the membrane pores, caused by concentration polarization, can lead to pore blockage and reduced membrane flux [51]. Therefore, guaranteeing the long-term stability of NF membrane is crucial to minimize manufacturing costs and maintain optimal performance. To examine the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane’s performance in terms of flux and retention of Li+ and Mg2+, a continuous operation of a nanofiltration membrane flux device was conducted for 120 h, as shown in Fig. 9
. This assessment provides valuable insights into the membrane's durability and sustained efficiency. Taking TFC-2 membrane as an example, the separation factor SLi, Mg substantially decreased but remained higher than that of TFC membrane during 120 h of continuous filtration. Minor decline in membrane flux occurred, attributable to fouling, yet without major fluctuations. Throughout the duration of the experiment, the TFC-2 membrane demonstrated consistent selectivity and durability, as observed from the long-term test results. The membrane maintained its performance without any significant deviations or degradation over time, highlighting its reliability for sustained separation processes. The enhanced stability of the nanofiltration membrane can be linked to the strengthened chemical bonding resulting from the reaction between γ-CD and remaining chloride groups from TMC. This reaction increased the cross-linking within the active layer, leading to a denser structure. Additionally, the abundant hydroxyl groups of γ-CDs formed hydrogen bonding interactions with the OCO bond on the PES substrate, strengthening the interfacial bonding between the substrate and the selective layer. This interplay of chemical bonds and hydrogen bonding contributed to the membrane's overall durability, ensuring its sustained performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pore size distribution of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 10, we have thoroughly characterized the pore size features of the nanofiltration membrane using the MWCO and pore size distribution diagrams. Our research results indicate that the MWCOs of TMC and TMC-2 are respectively 514 Da and 399 Da, which verifies that the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane we prepared falls within the nanofiltration category [15,52]. The grafting of γ-CD enhanced the cross-linking degree of the PA layer, which subsequently resulted in a reduction of the membrane pore size. As illustrated in Fig. 10 (b), the PEI-TMC membrane exhibits a broad pore size distribution with an average pore size of 0.58 nm. However, after grafting, this size reduces to 0.52 nm. This adjustment in pore size sheds light on the superior separation performance of the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane under elevated SLi, Mg values, with its permeation properties remaining stable, albeit with a slight decrease [3,37]. Furthermore, the match between the γ-CD pore size and the hydrated diameter of ions, in conjunction with our added MWCO and pore size distribution data, provides further validation for the separation mechanism [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of separation in the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To further elucidate the separation mechanism, we conducted a comparative analysis of its separation efficiency for Li+ and Mg2+ ions with other NF membranes documented in existing studies, as listed in Table 3 . Remarkably, the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane demonstrated superior separation capabilities compared to the membranes reported in previous studies. Notably, when compared to our previous work [33], the γ-CD-PEI-TMC membrane obtained through surface grafting technique exhibited significantly enhanced performance in the Li+/Mg2+ separation.

The remarkable separation efficiency of the γ-CD-grafted membrane is attributed to its unique surface structure, depicted in Fig. 11 . The incprporation of γ-CD contributes to the optimal separation performance through several key factors. On one hand, previous investigations [33] have shown that the internal cavity dimensions of γ-CD is in the range of 0.76–0.83 nm, which closely corresponds to the hydrated diameters of Mg2+ and Li+ ions (0.86 nm and 0.76 nm). This implies that the γ-CD cavity size was between the hydrated sizes of Mg2+ and Li+ ions, enabling rapid passage for Li+ while impeding the passage of Mg2+. On the other hand, γ-CD, as a versatile and flexible host molecule, has the ability to facilitate the optimal binding of metal cations through metal-linked inclusion complexation [30]. The binding selectivity and strength of the γ-CD-cation complex are determined by factors such as the metal cation's charge density and electronic configuration of the metal cation, along with the number of coordination sites in its CD complex [30–32]. Thus, the host–guest interaction mechanism mainly depends on the metal cation itself, where higher charge density results in stronger affinity with γ-CD [32]. γ-CD possesses a typical cavity structure, allowing it to form inclusion compounds with various guest molecules. The obtained binding constant is a critical parameter for assessing the stability of an inclusion compound. In this study, the binding constant of γ-CD with Mg2+ and Li+ was determined using 1H NMR spectroscopy (presented in Fig. S3
,
Fig. S4
, and
Table S1 ). The affinity constant for the γ-CD-Mg2+ complex was found to be 1 × 103 M−1. However, no conclusive results could be obtained for the γ-CD-Li+ complex as there were no observable shifts in the 1H NMR spectrum. This could be attributed to the possibility that the binding constant for the γ-CD-Li+ complex is too low to be accurately determined using 1H NMR spectroscopy [38]. Consequently, Mg2+ exhibited higher binding abilities with γ-CD than Li+. From this perspective, Li+ was more easily able to pass through the membrane modified by γ-CDs compared to Mg2+, providing an explanation for the high Mg-Li coefficient in this work. Therefore, the surface grafting of γ-CDs onto the NF membrane, rather than their physical blending or use as reactants in the IP reaction, led to an accumulation of γ-CDs cavities on the surface of membrane, which enhanced the magnesium-lithium separation performance.

To summarize, the current research effectively fabricated a new NF membrane by incorporating γ-CD, which introduced a distinct pore structure characterized by hydrophobic cavities and hydrophilic exteriors. This incorporation greatly improved the water affinity of the membrane surface. Zeta potential assessments confirmed the consumption of residual acyl chloride groups by γ-CD, resulting in a weak electropositive charge on the membrane surface under neutral conditions. The incorporation of γ-CD led to a remarkable reduction (from 61° down to 18.5°) in the water contact angle, consequently enhancing the hydrophilicity and permeability of the composite NF membrane. Additionally, the selectivity of the γ-CD cavities played a crucial role in enhancing the discrimination efficiency of the composite NF membrane with regards to Mg2+ and Li+ ions. The cavities of γ-CD acted as binding sites for the formation of metal-linked inclusion complexes with Mg2+ or Li+ ions. Notably, γ-CD exhibited a higher affinity for binding with Mg2+ compared to Li+. Under optimized experimental conditions, the composite NF membrane achieved an impressive separation coefficient of up to 23.61 and a water flux of 2.95 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1. These findings collectively demonstrate the significant potential of the composite NF membrane, with its unique morphology and properties, for enhancing the discriminative ability in the division of Mg2+ and Li+ ions. This highlights its promising application in the reclamation of lithium from brines.

Nan Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Tiancan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Weihao Xue: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Ying Zhao: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Bo Zhu: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyuan Pei: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhiwei Xu: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We express our profound gratitude to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22108203) for their significant financial support, which immensely contributed to the successful completion of this research project. Furthermore, we express our gratitude to the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for providing access to their cutting-edge facilities, including Scanning Electron Microscope, and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy, which significantly facilitated our experimental analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.125206.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
